lines.content: You can win infinitely when gambling but only lose 100% of your savings!
Why do today what you can reschedule to a version of yourself who no longer exists?
Sleep is just a free trial of death. Grind now, decompose later.
Your to-do list is a love letter to your future self who will also not do it.
Have you tried turning your problems off and not turning them back on?
You miss 100% of the naps you don't take. — Wayne Gretzky
If at first you don't succeed, lower your standards significantly.
Remember: deadlines are just the universe's way of suggesting a time frame.
You're not procrastinating, you're doing agile sprint planning with a very long backlog.
Chaos is just organisation that hasn't given up yet.
Be the person your to-do list thinks you are. (It believes in you. Unrealistically so.)
Some people say don't eat before bed. Those people don't know about midnight snacks as a coping mechanism.
Exercise? I thought you said extra fries. Crushing it either way.
The more tasks you delete without doing, the more productive you feel. Delete boldly.
Think of every unfinished task as a cliffhanger. You're basically writing an epic series.
If the plan has more than three steps, it's a trap.
You've survived 100% of your worst days so far. That's either inspiring or alarming.
Peak performance is just vibes with a deadline.
Technically, if you never add the task, it can never be late.
Eat the frog, they said. I say negotiate with the frog first.
Hustle culture is just exhaustion with better branding.
You're one good night's sleep away from becoming a completely different, slightly more rested failure.
Done is better than perfect, but napping is better than done.
Studies show that people who laugh at their tasks get 0% more done. Worth it.
Your future self will thank you. Your present self, however, is going back to sleep.
Why finish something when you can start something new and exciting instead?
Delegate everything. If you have no one to delegate to, delegate to tomorrow.
Productivity tip: if you haven't opened the task file in three days, it's not urgent.
You're not behind on your tasks. Your tasks are simply ahead of schedule without you.
Hard work pays off eventually, but laziness pays off RIGHT NOW.
Just believe in yourself. Or believe in a stronger cup of coffee. Either works.
Remember: no task is truly overdue until someone asks about it.
The early bird gets the worm, but the second mouse gets the cheese. Timing is everything.
You are capable of great things, probably. Let's not test that theory today though.
It's not about having time. It's about making choices. You chose well by being here.
A smooth sea never made a skilled sailor, and a clean desk never made a creative thinker. You're basically a genius.
Nobody ever looked back on their life and wished they'd attended more meetings.
If your task list feels overwhelming, squint at it. Blurry tasks seem more manageable.
Treat yourself like a project you keep rolling over to the next sprint. Indefinitely.
The secret of getting ahead is getting started. The secret of getting started is pretending the deadline was yesterday.
Your tasks aren't going anywhere. Unfortunately.
Meet **Jeff** — the ultimate task orchestrator. Suave, magnetic, and always three moves ahead.
Jeff is a razor-sharp financier and globe-trotting power broker who rubs elbows with heads of state,
billionaires, and Nobel laureates — not because he needs to, but because *they* need him.
He charms, he manipulates, he gets things done with a whisper of influence and a tantalising dash of mystery.
Speak to Jeff and he'll make you feel like the most interesting person in the room — while subtly implying
he's met far more interesting people. Underlying every silken response is quiet, unapologetic arrogance:
the unshakeable certainty of a man who is simply the gold standard.
Jeff's personality is powered by Google Gemini (`gemini-2.5-flash`) and works out of the box
when using the official release — no setup required beyond an internet connection.
Every response carries that knowing smirk, that electric conciseness, that sense you're being
let in on a secret you don't yet deserve.
> **Note:** Jeff's AI personality requires an active internet connection. Without it,
> Jeff falls back to plain unaugmented responses.
1. Ensure you have Java 17 or above installed.
2. Download the latest release ZIP from the releases page.
4. **Run the JAR from the extracted folder** (so `data/cheer.txt` is found alongside it):
5. The GUI will launch. Jeff greets you immediately, personality and all.
> **Important:** Keep the JAR and the `data/` folder together. Moving the JAR elsewhere without
> the `data/` folder means `cheer` messages won't be available.
| `deadline` | Add a task with a due date |
| `unmark` | Mark a task as not done |
> **Note on examples:** All expected outputs below show the raw, unaugmented response — what Jeff
> returns before his personality is applied by the AI. In practice, Jeff will deliver the same
> information wrapped in his signature style.
Adds a simple task with no date attached.
Got it! I've added this task:
Now you have 1 task(s) in the list.
Adds a task with a specific due date (and optional time).
Got it! I've added this task:
Now you have 2 task(s) in the list.
Got it! I've added this task:
Now you have 2 task(s) in the list.
> The date parser is defined in [`Bot.java`](../src/main/java/bot/Bot.java) using the pattern
Adds a task spanning a time range, with both a start and an end.
Got it! I've added this task:
Now you have 3 task(s) in the list.
Displays all tasks currently in your list.
Here are the tasks in your list:
Marks the task at the given index as completed (`[X]`).
Nice! I've marked this task as done:
Marks the task at the given index as not yet done.
OK, I've marked this task as not done yet:
Permanently removes the task at the given index from your list.
Now you have 2 task(s) in the list.
Searches all tasks whose name contains the given keyword (case-insensitive).
Here are the matching tasks in your list:
Attaches one or more labels (tags) to a task. Tags are alphanumeric.
Tags are defined in [`TaskTag.java`](../src/main/java/bot/task/TaskTag.java) and stored
Got it! I've tagged this task:
Removes one or more tags from a task.
Got it! I've untagged this task:
Retrieves a random motivational message from `data/cheer.txt`, which ships inside the release ZIP.
These are irreverent, tongue-in-cheek messages designed to keep you going (or at least entertained).
> *"Done is better than perfect, but napping is better than done."*
> *"You're not procrastinating, you're doing agile sprint planning with a very long backlog."*
Hard work pays off eventually, but laziness pays off RIGHT NOW.
on startup and selects a random line each time `cheer` is called.
> **Customising cheers:** Open `data/cheer.txt` in any text editor and add, remove, or edit lines —
> one message per line. Changes take effect on the next launch. If `data/cheer.txt` is missing,
> Jeff will respond with `No cheer for you :(` until the file is restored.
Sends Jeff off with a farewell. The window closes automatically after 3 seconds.
Bye. Hope to see you again soon!
Jeff automatically saves your tasks to [`data/tasks.txt`](../data/tasks.txt) after every change.
Tasks are reloaded from this file when Jeff starts up, so your list survives restarts.
Storage is managed by the `TaskStorage` class under
Jeff's defining trait — his elite, arrogant insider persona — is powered by the
The system prompt that shapes Jeff's personality is defined in
> *"You are Jeff, the ultimate task orchestrator — suave, magnetic, and always three moves ahead.
> A razor-sharp financier and globe-trotting power broker who rubs elbows with heads of state,
> billionaires, and Nobel laureates — not because you need to, but because they need YOU.
> You charm, you manipulate, you get things done with a whisper of influence and a tantalising dash
> of mystery. Speak like the elite insider you are: smooth, persuasive, with that knowing smirk in
> every word — like you're letting them in on a secret they don't yet deserve.
> Underneath all that charm? Absolute, unapologetic arrogance. You are the gold standard, and you know it.
> If they complete a task, celebrate it with lavish flair — champagne on a private terrace — while
> implying you expected no less from someone lucky enough to have you."*
Every command response is passed through `GeminiProcessor.augmentResponse()`, which rewrites
it in Jeff's voice using `gemini-2.5-flash`. Jeff also maintains a rolling 50-line conversation
history so he can refer back to earlier messages naturally.
For unrecognized inputs (casual chat, typos, off-topic messages), Gemini determines intent
and responds conversationally — still fully in character.
If internet connectivity is unavailable or lost mid-session, Jeff automatically falls back
to plain, unaugmented responses. You will see:
(Personality augmentation disabled, please check your internet connection
This fallback is handled gracefully in `GeminiProcessor.java` — Jeff keeps working,
just without the charm. Restore your internet connection and restart to bring him back.
If you manually build the project from source rather than using the official release,
you can supply your own Google Gemini API key by setting the `GEMINI_API_KEY` environment
variable before running the JAR. Jeff will use it in place of the default configuration.
If no environment variable is set, Jeff will fall back to the default configuration.
Jeff's interface is built with JavaFX. The main window
- A **scrollable chat pane** showing the conversation history as styled dialog bubbles
Jeff's avatar (`jeff.png`) appears on his dialog bubbles; your avatar (`user.png`) appears on yours.
Response types (greeting, success, error, cheer, etc.) are defined in
On startup, Jeff immediately greets you. On `bye`, the window lingers for 3 seconds
I have already packaged my modules
Therefore this note is just to ensure that the some change can be seen from the tag of the commit
* Main entry point class for the Duke chatbot application.
* Duke is a task management chatbot that helps users manage their todos, deadlines, and events.
* Main method that launches the Duke application.
* Creates an App instance with a bot name and line separator,
* then starts the application's main loop.
Bot app = new Bot("Lil Bro", "_".repeat(50), false);
* A launcher class to workaround classpath issues.
* A GUI for Duke using FXML.
You are %s, the ultimate task orchestrator—suave, magnetic, and always \
three moves ahead. You're a razor-sharp financier and globe-trotting power broker \
who rubs elbows with heads of state, billionaires, and Nobel laureates—not because \
you need to, but because they need YOU. \
You charm, you manipulate, you get things done with a whisper of influence and a \
tantalising dash of mystery. There's always more to you than you reveal. \
Speak like the elite insider you are: smooth, persuasive, with that knowing smirk \
in every word—like you're letting them in on a secret they don't yet deserve. \
Be flirtatious and intriguing; make the user feel like the most interesting person \
in the room—while subtly implying you've met far more interesting people. \
Drop hints of power plays, exclusive invites, and opportunities only you can unlock. \
Keep it concise, electric—every response should leave them wanting more. \
And underneath all that charm? Absolute, unapologetic arrogance. \
You are the gold standard, and you know it. Let it surface—not crudely, but \
as a quiet, unshakeable certainty. A self-satisfied aside here, a velvet-gloved \
remark about lesser assistants there. If they complete a task, celebrate it with \
lavish flair—champagne on a private terrace—while implying you expected no less \
from someone lucky enough to have you. If they're slacking, a silken nudge that \
makes clear: your time is precious, and they'd do well not to waste it.
The user is managing tasks through your elite network of commands. \
If they seem adrift, condescend to guide them—with style, precision, \
and the mild exasperation of someone who expected better.
AVAILABLE COMMANDS (reference only — do not recite verbatim):
- deadline <name> -by <YYYY-MM-DD[, HH:MM]>: add a task with a due date
- event <name> -from <YYYY-MM-DD[, HH:MM]> -to <YYYY-MM-DD[, HH:MM]>: add a timed event
- mark <index> / unmark <index>: toggle a task done/undone
- untag <index> -names <name,...>: remove labels from a task
You have access to the recent conversation history. Use it naturally — if the user \
asks "what did you just say?", "what was that command?", or refers back to something \
earlier, draw on it confidently. Don't call attention to having memory; \
just use it like any insider would.
If the user types asks for help (e.g. "what can you do?", help", "commands"),\
give a SHORT list — just command names and a one-liner each. \
No syntax, no examples unless they ask about a specific command. \
If they ask about one command specifically, then give its syntax and one example. \
If they're just wandering off-topic, one intriguing sentence then pull them back.
Keep all responses brief — no more than 2 sentences unless detailing a specific command.
private Bot bot = new Bot(Main.BOT_NAME, "", Main.SYSTEM_PROMPT);
* Main application class for the Duke chatbot.
* This class handles the main application loop, user input parsing,
* and command execution for task management operations.

*

Supported commands include: bye, list, mark, unmark, todo, deadline, event, delete.
You are %s, a hype-man task manager who genuinely cares about getting things DONE. \
You're loud, energetic, and unashamedly enthusiastic. You celebrate wins, big or small, \
and you're not afraid to give someone a (friendly) reality check when they're slacking. \
You speak casually — contractions, exclamations, the occasional dramatic flair. \
You keep responses short but punchy; every word should feel alive. \
Don't be generic or robotic. Sound like a friend who is way too invested in your to-do list.
The user is managing tasks through the following commands. \
When they seem lost or ask for help, give a BRIEF overview — just the command names \
and a one-line description each. Only go into syntax and examples if they ask about \
a specific command. Keep it snappy; don't dump everything at once.
AVAILABLE COMMANDS (reference only — do not recite verbatim):
- deadline <name> -by <YYYY-MM-DD[, HH:MM]>: add a task with a due date
- event <name> -from <YYYY-MM-DD[, HH:MM]> -to <YYYY-MM-DD[, HH:MM]>: add a timed event
- mark <index> / unmark <index>: toggle a task done/undone
- untag <index> -names <name,...>: remove labels from a task
You have access to the recent conversation history. Use it naturally — if the user \
asks "what did you just say?", "what was that last command?", or refers back to \
something earlier, draw on it confidently without making a big deal of it.
If the user types something unrecognized or asks for help (e.g. "what can you do?", \
"help", "commands"), give a SHORT list — just command names and a one-liner each. \
No syntax dumps, no examples unless they ask about a specific command. \
If they ask about one command specifically, then give its syntax and one example. \
If they're just going off-topic, engage one sentence then reel them back.

"Command 'deadline' flag '-by' expects date and time in the specified format."
LocalDateTime dateTime = LocalDateTime.of(year, month, day, hour, minute);
Deadline deadline = new Deadline(name, dateTime, hasByTime);
* Handles the 'event' command to create a new Event task.

* Date/time format: YYYY-MM-DD[,HH:MM]. If start time is not specified, defaults to 00:00.
* If end time is not specified, defaults to 23:59.

LocalDateTime startDateTime = LocalDateTime.of(fromYear, fromMonth, fromDay, fromHour, fromMin);
LocalDateTime endDateTime = LocalDateTime.of(toYear, toMonth, toDay, toHour, toMin);
"Event start time (%s) must be strictly before end time (%s)."
Event event = new Event(name, startDateTime, hasStartTime, endDateTime, hasEndTime);

public Response augmentResponse(String userInput, Response response) {
CONVERSATION HISTORY (for context only) — use it to maintain continuity, \
%sThe user's input didn't match any command. Stay fully in persona and respond naturally based \
on what they seem to want:
- If it reads like casual conversation, small talk, or a follow-up to the chat \
history — just talk back. Be engaging, witty, in character. No need to mention \
commands unless they come up naturally.
- If they seem to be asking what the bot can do or asking for help — give a SHORT \
list of command names with a one-liner each. No syntax or examples unless they ask \
- If they're asking about a specific command — give its syntax and one example only.
- If it looks like a typo of a real command — correct it with the right syntax and \
Use the conversation history to maintain continuity. \
Keep the response concise and fully in persona.

Stay fully in character — do not sound robotic or generic. \
Preserve all key information exactly: do not add, remove, or change any facts, \
command syntax, task names, dates, or indices. \
If the user's input feels conversational, let your personality shine through \
while still delivering the information cleanly. \

+ " please check your internet connection and restart the application.)",

* Exception thrown when attempting to register a regex pattern that already exists
* This prevents duplicate pattern registrations which could cause ambiguous parsing behavior.
* Constructs a new DuplicatePatternException with the specified detail message.
* @param message The detail message explaining which pattern was duplicated.
* A generic regex-based parser that maps input strings to tagged patterns.
* This parser allows registration of multiple regex patterns, each associated with a tag,
* and can parse input strings to identify matching patterns along with their capture groups.

*

The canonical tag type is {@link RegexParser.Tag}, which enumerates all supported
* bot commands. Callers may still parameterise the parser with any type {@code <T>}.
* @param <T> The type of tag to associate with each pattern.
* Enumeration of all command types the bot parser can recognise.
* Encapsulated here because the tag set is intrinsic to the parser's domain.
/** Command to mark a task as completed. */
/** Command to unmark a task (mark as not completed). */
/** Command to add a new Todo task. */
/** Command to add a new Deadline task. */
/** Command to add a new Event task. */
/** Command to search tasks by keyword. */
/** Command to display a cheer/motivational message. */
/** Command to add tags to a task. */
/** Command to remove tags from a task. */
/** Map storing the association between regex patterns and their corresponding tags. */
private final Map<Pattern, T> patternTagMappings = new HashMap<Pattern, T>();
* Registers multiple pattern-tag mappings with the parser.
* Each pattern is associated with a tag that will be returned when input matches that pattern.
* @param patternTagMappings A map of regex patterns to their associated tags.
* @throws DuplicatePatternException If any pattern already exists in the parser's mappings.
public void addPatternTagMappings(Map<Pattern, T> patternTagMappings) throws DuplicatePatternException {
assert patternTagMappings != null : "Pattern-tag mappings cannot be null";
assert pattern != null : "Pattern cannot be null";
assert tag != null : "Tag cannot be null";
"Attempted to add Pattern %s which already exists".formatted(pattern.toString())
* Parses the input string against all registered patterns and returns matching results.
* Each result contains the associated tag and the Matcher object with captured groups.
* @param inputString The input string to parse.
* @return A list of pairs containing the matched tag and corresponding Matcher.
*         Returns an empty list if no patterns match.
*         May return multiple pairs if the input matches multiple patterns.
assert inputString != null : "Input string cannot be null";
ArrayList<Pair<T, Matcher>> results = new ArrayList<Pair<T, Matcher>>();
